name: 3 Bottled Fish
description: Once only a Stonestown Farmers Market stand, 3 Bottled Fish now has a compact restaurant in Oakland’s Fruitvale neighborhood. Chef and owner Paulette Tran keeps a tight menu, which changes weekly. Bánh mì ($12) are a fixture; Tran might stuff crunchy baguettes with stacks of house-cured sausage or braised chicken. Equally enticing are the recurring crackly salmon belly ($14) accompanied by rice, and the soulful rice porridge ($11) fragrant with ground beef, ginger and green onions. For something lighter, try the bun bo xao ($16), a beef noodle salad with a side of piquant nước chấm. Get takeout or sit indoors, which is styled with greenery and embroidery by local artists.   Also featured on Top Vietnamese Restaurants.

name: Aphotic
description: This is the last time Aphotic will appear on our Top 25 list; it will serve its final sea lettuce croissant on Dec. 21. The seafood-centric restaurant offers 11- ($215) and 16-course ($295) tasting menus in its expansive, shadowy dining room, which makes you feel like Jonah in the belly of a whale. There are also a la carte options available at the bar, which is anchored by a wall of glass separatory funnels — very AP Chem chic — filled with house-made distillates. As you might imagine, the cocktail program is a highlight, but teetotalers are also taken care of with the wildly creative no-ABV “sea level pairing.” Visit while you still can.  Also featured on Top Fine Dining Restaurants and Top San Francisco Restaurants.

name: Aziza
description: You could describe Aziza as a neighborhood restaurant. On a recent evening, the clientele — a young couple on what seemed to be an awkward second date, a quartet of seniors casually dressed — seemed primed for a low-key Sunday night rather than a dining event. But Aziza started serving high-key delicious Moroccan food back in 1999, and even if you don’t live in the Richmond, it is absolutely worth the trip to the Avenues. Chef Mourad Lahlou’s straightforward classics like the savory-sweet basteeya ($28), confited chicken wrapped in a pastry parcel, will never leave the menu. But roll the dice on more creative dishes like the mussels ($20), drenched in herbs and vadouvan butter and broiled on the half shell. You’ll be amply rewarded. Also featured on Top San Francisco Restaurants.

name: Barberio Osteria
description: From the same team behind AltoVino, Barberio Osteria is a new gem offering first-rate handmade pastas and seasonally driven, Italian-inflected dishes. Less than a year old, it goes toe-to-toe with the stalwarts of the local Italian scene. Anchovy fans will flip for the bagna cauda ($16) an infused olive oil dip served warm over a votive candle with a selection of raw and nearly raw veggies. Produce is in capable hands; saucy veal short ribs ($46) were unsurprisingly delicious, but they were nearly upstaged by grilled mini eggplant. For dessert, try the zabaglione, as frothy as hair mousse and escorted, recently, by the season’s first stone fruit. Also featured on Top Italian Restaurants.

name: Four Kings
description: Is there a German word for being sucked into someone else’s nostalgia so fully that you start to experience it too? Even if you didn’t grow up eating pork chop rice at a casual cha chaan teng in Hong Kong, you might feel a tingle of homesickness midway through the version at Four Kings, which is served with a dynamite tomato gravy thickened with egg ($29). This is not to say the menu is all greatest Cantonese hits; owners Mike Long and Franky Ho, who met while cooking at Mister Jiu’s, would be bored with that. And so there’s mapo spaghetti ($21), reminiscent of Cincinnati chili but with a wallop of spice, and XO escargot ($24). This is food best eaten at the izakaya-style counter, washed down with beer or one of the house shochu highballs. Do a shot with your neighbor and, if you speak Cantonese, sing along to the ’90s Cantopop playlist. Also featured on Top San Francisco Restaurants.

name: Friends and Family
description: Between the lauded beverage program and community events like Queer Speed Dating, you could conceivably become a regular at Friends and Family, one of North America’s 50 Best Bars, without ever having a meal. But that would be a mistake. Chef Gaby Maeda, who took the reins earlier this year, previously ran the kitchen at State Bird Provisions, and her playful and personal cooking catapults Friends and Family beyond “bar with good snacks” territory. The “les eggs” ($12), a riff on oeufs mayonnaise, are appropriately named for Frenchy eggs served at a quasi-lesbian bar. Save room for Mom’s carrot cake ($9), which owner Blake Cole’s mother has been making since Friends and Family opened its doors in 2020.

name: Joodooboo
description: Despite debuting two years ago, Joodooboo has emerged as a one-of-a-kind Korean restaurant in Oakland for its focus on the cuisine’s gentler side. Seasonal banchan and handmade tofu are the restaurant’s bread and butter. The former is featured prominently over the farmer’s bowl ($18), a rice and lettuce plate crowned with that week’s offerings — currently corn and zucchini swirled with tofu and marinated eggplants with sungold tomatoes. Or try the best of both worlds with the incredibly crispy, ornate fried dooboo set ($20), a neat collection of side dishes, a soup and fried tofu. For a more substantial meal, check out the fish specials, like broiled ($15) or fried cod ($15), served with a piquant, tangy wine and chile-garlic sauce. Sip on an effervescent spritz ($6) made with fruit puree, like spicy watermelon or salted cucumber. Indoor and outdoor seating available.  Also featured on Top Korean Restaurants.

name: Khao Tiew
description: Khao Tiew has made West Portal a destination for Thai cooking with fun, remixed versions of popular dishes. The family-friendly restaurant has a dining room decorated with vintage Thai ads and movie posters. Start off with fried papaya salad ($14), a distant relative to the blooming onion, where the fruit is fried and served with the dressing on the side. Do not miss the short rib fried rice ($20) with cured egg yolk, which radiates with beef tallow richness. The duck panang curry ($26) is aromatic, mellow and creamy, and the branzino ($36) is sweet, tender and juicy. The kaya toast ($15), a pan-seared slice of shokupan topped with pastel green kaya and a scoop of ice cream, is incredible stuff. Do not miss it.

name: La Gastronomia
description: Tucked away in the hippie Marin County town of Fairfax, La Gastronomia stands out for its rustic Tuscan cooking, with freshly made, rib-sticking pastas. Inside, sleek marble countertops and tables flirt with luxury, and the outdoor street-facing wood benches are ideal for sunny brunch. Dinner kicks off with complimentary focaccia, which is spongy and faintly sweet, like a savory pound cake. Red sauce-lovers should seek out the rich, mildly spicy amatriciana bucatini ($28), while white sauce fans should order the creamy mushroom pappardelle ($30), topped with shaved black truffles. For lunch, opt for the succulent porchetta sandwich ($19) or a build-your-own-pasta ($22), choosing your shape and sauce. Also featured on Top Italian Restaurants.

name: La Jacaranda Oaxaqueña
description: Located in an open-air food wonderland in San Jose, food truck La Jacaranda Oaxaqueña stands out with its tlayudas ($21), large thin tortillas smothered with rich beans and other toppings, which La Jacaranda prepares folded over instead of open-faced. Its edge over the competition is a charcoal-burning grill that imparts the tlayudas with smokiness and turns the tortilla crisp. For lighter fare, try the memelitas ($5), corn saucers topped with avocado leaf-perfumed beans and meat. The plentiful seating is outdoors at La Jacaranda, under tall canopy protection. Also featured on Top Outdoor Dining Restaurants.

name: Lunette
description: After closing Nyum Bai in Oakland, chef Nite Yun has returned with Lunette, a casual counter-service Cambodian restaurant that makes an excellent addition to the Ferry’s Building dining roster. Red pendant lights and a vibrant hand-painted mural, made to look like colorful Khmer woven mats, create a charming environment. Yun’s mission of championing Khmer cuisine remains; fans of the chef will recognize the Kuy Teav Phnom Penh ($21), a shrimp and pork noodle dish with a soulful broth. Don’t sleep on the new additions like tender shaking beef ($21) or crackly fried chicken ($16), both of which are warmed with a heavy dosage of black pepper. Dine-in only, but if you decide to do takeout there’s public seating outside of the Ferry Building.

name: Mägo
description: Oakland’s fine dining scene is limited, but among the ranks is the wonderful Mago, where chef Mark Liberman explores his Colombian heritage on the plate. Mago is laid-back, with colorful walls that resemble Colombia’s colorways, while dinner is sonically paired with a soundtrack of Latin American pop hits. The tasting menu (weekdays for $82, weekends for $110) consists of 10 courses, where South American staples like arepas and yuca become elegant vehicles for seasonality. Main courses involve the flavor of fire, like juicy swordfish or tender steak, grilled over a steering wheel-operated wood flame. Consider opting for the drink pairing ($70), which includes a surprisingly diverse selection of wines, sakes and cocktails, and succeeds at bringing out fun subtleties in the food.

name: Mijoté
description: The chef is Japanese and the space is a former sushi restaurant, but Mijoté is the closest you’ll come to dining in contemporary Paris. Co-owner Kosuke Tada was the chef at Le 6 Paul Bert, a neo-bistro that’s a darling of the international, natural wine guzzling, cool-kid culinary set. His four-course set menu ($82) changes at least weekly and melds technique with the season’s bounty. On a recent visit, a first course of luscious tuna crudo was paired with crunchy snap peas and their tendrils, jewel-toned strawberries, capers and fresh horseradish — a textural masterpiece. Portions tend to be generous, but you still might find yourself tempted to add a supplementary course, so good do they sound, or the cheese of the day ($12), served with Josey Baker bread and a house-made accompaniment like candied lemon peel. By the glass or by the bottle, the wine list, with a focus on small producers who keep intervention to a minimum, is a ripper.  Also featured on Top French Restaurants.

name: Minnie Bell’s Soul Movement
description: After six years of operating at an Emeryville food court, Minnie Bell’s Soul Movement is finally in its rightful place, San Francisco’s Fillmore district, where chef and owner Fernay McPherson grew up and long dreamed of opening a full-service version of her restaurant. You can still find her unmatched mac and cheese ($5 for small, $13 for large), a dream-like dish that’s a perfect Venn diagram of gooey cheese, seasoning and soft pasta. The bechamel is fortified with a blend of Parmesan, cheddar and fontina then folded into elbow macaroni. The showstopping herby fried chicken (single piece for $4.50; two piece combo for $20), fragrant with rosemary, is not to be missed. Pair the crunchy chicken with homemade hot sauce ($3) and a cold beer or glass of wine — all new additions to the restaurant. Also featured on Top Mac and Cheese and Top Fried Chicken.

name: Ok’s Deli
description: After much anticipation, Ok’s Deli (pronounced Oak’s) transitioned from a pop-up to a small sandwich shop in North Oakland in 2022. Chef Albert Ok pays homage to the Asian American experience through the deli format, with a fine dining pedigree. A Spam-stuffed bánh mi, Sichuan peppercorn-dusted hot chicken ($17) and a pig head sisig bolillo sandwich ($16) are but a few of the fantastic, creative and Asian-inspired options you might encounter. Despite these modern entries, the deli also leaves room for more traditional options like turkey or Italian. There are a few mainstays, but the menu evolves with the weather: If it’s cold outside, you’re more likely to see hot sandwiches. There are a few tables on the sidewalk, if you want to eat there. Also featured on Top Sandwiches.

name: Outta Sight Pizza
description: Outta Sight stunned popup diners at wine bar Fig & Thistle with its thin New York-style pizzas before opening a place of its own in late 2022. The white-walled slice shop is full of skater attitude, with a painting of rapper Mos Def, framed action-shot photos of skaters and a boombox. Mainstay slices like pepperoni ($5.50), sausage and peppers ($5.50), and puttanesca ($5.25) command respect with crunch and tangy tomato sauce. Looking for extra pizazz? Every slice has the option of complimentary shaved cheese. Whole pies are available, too ($30-$37). While those are fantastic, Outta Sight also dabbles in the sandwich arts. The standout options are the gooey Morty Melt ($11), a mortadella sandwich gussied up with cheese and pesto, and an Italian hoagie ($17) stacked with meats, basil mayo and vinegar-drenched veggies. Also featured on Top Sandwiches, Top San Francisco Restaurants and Top Pizza.

name: Pho Duoi Bo
description: Kobe beef cooked under a raging torch flame might be the draw at Pho Duoi Bo in San Jose, a Northern Vietnamese beef pho specialist, where everything on the menu calls back to the cow. Located in an Eastside strip mall, Pho Duoi Bo is a chill environment decorated with murals of Hanoi’s landmarks and a bucking steer. Of course, you have to try the Kobe beef (side for $11.99), which adds a toasty, buttery tenderness to any bowl of pho; you can order it raw on the side or in the bowl ($21.95), if you prefer. While fancy beef might be the marquee item, the succulent oxtail ($21.95) and overall execution of beef is why you return to the restaurant.

name: Popoca
description: Oakland’s Popoca is changing the way the Bay Area thinks about Salvadoran food. Chef Anthony Salguero has created a relaxing environment with plenty of swagger and a thoughtful menu that calls back to tradition in one moment, then shatters those expectations in the next. Pupusas are tricked-out, made with heirloom corn, cooked by a toasty wood flame and filled with seasonal produce like roasted Jimmy Nardello peppers and salted fish. The restaurant has a host of killer snacks like yuca topped with marinated anchovies, a smooth, savory bean tamal and plums topped with tamarind granita and powdered pumpkin seeds. You must order a cocktail — or at least sip on some Central American rum — from Eduardo “Ed” Peraza Flores’ sensational beverage program, which is full of nods to El Salvador.

name: Psychic Pie
description: The small Wine Country city of Sebastopol is home to Psychic Pie, a fun pizzeria that has a fresh take on the Roman style with divine sourdough. Mechanically, it functions like a slice shop, only the sheet pan pies are cut with scissors and sold by weight. Pepperoni (slice for about $6.70 or $24 per pound), sausage with pickled onions (slice for about $6.50 or $25 per pound) and mushroom (slice for about $6.60 or $22 per pound) are among the more traditional topping options, while the off-the-wall combinations pair cured meats and seasonal produce, like one that features asparagus, salami and pimento cheese (slice for about $6.70 or $24 per pound). For extra oomph, each slice is garnished with shaved cheese, herbs or a drizzle of olive oil. End the meal with thick buffalo milk soft serve ($5), sometimes presented in a mug with a freaky winking face. The easygoing pizzeria, with its pink-and-white walls and psychic medium mural, offers both indoor and outdoor seating.  Also featured on Top Pizza.

name: Rich Table
description: It’s no secret that, 12 years in, Rich Table remains one of the best restaurants in San Francisco. The energy within the dimly lit, rustic dining room is palpable — the diners know they’re lucky to be there, and the servers exude conviviality. The sea urchin cacio e pepe pasta ($27) confidently claps back at purists who reject the pairing of seafood and cheese. It’s a stalwart of the menu from chefs Sarah and Evan Rich, which otherwise bends and flexes to showcase whatever produce is currently shining. During citrus season, that might mean a dessert of passion fruit and guava sorbets blanketed by fluffy mandarin ice and sweetened condensed milk ($14), like POG shave ice dressed up for a night out. Also featured on Top San Francisco Restaurants.

name: SingleThread
description: At fine dining restaurants, it’s common to begin with “a little gift from the chef.” This can take the form of a small amuse bouche or a parade of canapes. But at SingleThread, the three-Michelin-starred restaurant from chef Kyle and farmer Katina Connaughton, the opening salvo is less a little gift and more a new luxury sedan with an oversized bow. Small dishes of raw fish and precious produce are nestled on a hillock of moss and wildflowers installed in the center of your table. It’s a bounty of delights, and it’s only the first of 10 Cali-kaiseki courses. Hospitality is similarly over-the-top. Servers use hand gestures, like baseball catchers, to signal to colleagues which diners have ordered sparkling or still water. Every time you use the restroom, you’ll observe the toilet paper has been neatly folded into a triangle, like at a hotel. It all makes for a luxurious experience worth the high cost of entry ($440-$485 per person). Also featured on Top Fine Dining Restaurants.

name: Tacos Mama Cuca
description: Food truck Tacos Mama Cuca is a singular voice in the Bay Area’s formidable taco scene. Owner Maria Marquez started off selling handmade flour tortillas of unmatched quality, then opened a pandemic-era home restaurant so popular that the city of Oakland shut it down. With a new, fully permitted truck, Marquez and her husband, Juan Carlos Garcia, invite people into their slice of Sonora, Mexico. Garcia expertly grills carne asada to serve on Marquez’s pliable, translucent flour tortillas. Tacos ($5) are for those seeking purer flavors; try the crackly tripas or smoky al pastor. The taco yaqui ($12) is the star dish: a gigantic, decked-out taco stacked with a cheesy, scorched Anaheim pepper, spicy beans and smoky asada. But what puts Tacos Mama Cuca in a class of its own are the unmatched salsas: a cooling avocado sauce, a blazing hot habanero option and three chunky, smoke-infused molcajete salsas. Tamp the fire and smoke with a creamy cebada agua fresca, made with toasted barley. Also featured on Top Tacos.

name: Tanzie’s
description: Tanzie’s, a Thai brunch spot in Berkeley with a toasty greenhouse vibe, summons the flavors of Chiang Mai. The intricacies of Northern-style cooking are on display with fragrant, pungent sai ua ($8), house-made sausages flavored with herbs and spices. That fantastic link is sliced and paired with a runny, open-faced omelet ($22) and pepper relish, better known as nam prik noom. Try the sizzling skillets, your choice of stewed pork belly ($26) or rice swirled with spicy pork, also known as nam prik ong ($25). Make sure you try the beignets (three for $5, five for $7), which come with a spot of condensed milk for dipping. Outdoor seating available.

name: Valley Bar and Bottle
description: So charming is Valley that you might start fantasizing about moving to Sonoma just so you can become a regular. Open from breakfast straight through to dinner, the wine-centric restaurant and bottle shop will attend to your all-day needs, whether you want a nibble with a glass of pét nat (a tin of very good anchovies, $12) or a whole meal (a vinegary, paprikash-inspired half chicken, $38). An appetizer of gooey Brillat-Savarin with figs roasted in plenty of olive oil ($16) is indicative of what they do best — giving very good raw materials the help they need and nothing more. Take home a bottle of their house wine, released under the name Le Lube.  Also featured on Top Sonoma County Restaurants

name: Zuni Cafe
description: Is there a better way to spend a lazy Friday afternoon than at Zuni with a Bloody Mary, a burger ($22), a towering plate of shoestring fries ($11) and a quivering slice of Gâteau Victoire? If there is, let us know and we’ll meet you there. In the meantime, we’ll be basking in the ethereal light that streams through the windows of the idiosyncratic triangular space and bounces off the copper bar. Since it opened in 1979, Zuni has been a place where City Hall power brokers rub shoulders with gays about town and families celebrate birthdays. All of them consider the restaurant to be their personal special spot, and they’re all right.  Also featured on Top Burgers and Top Fries.

name: Burdell
description: You know the way some people feel about their city’s pennant-winning baseball team? That’s the way we feel about Burdell. Pride is a good word for it.   When critics from other cities come to town, we delight in watching their eyes roll back in their heads as they crunch their way through Burdell’s cheeky spin on chicken and waffles. When a New Orleans-based food writer declares the barbecue shrimp to be better than back home, our chests puff with pride.  Geoff Davis’ personal take on soul food is an ode to his grandmother, culinarily and stylistically. Drenched in amber tones, the interior has retro charm mixed with the intimacy of a grandparent’s living room — evidenced by a gallery wall of the staff’s family portraits. At Burdell, a stereo system from the ’70s makes music sound better, church pews tracing the walls create a sense of communal dining and the eye-catching lime green bar is just plain pretty.   Davis’ cooking expands the scope of soul food — a true American cuisine — by highlighting its often obscured agrarian roots and infusing it with a sense of place. In the winter, salads are composed of beets and California citrus. Collards are seasoned with berbere, threading together staples of the American South and the Horn of Africa. It might seem a little odd to be proud of a restaurant where we are merely patrons, but we couldn’t be more chuffed that Burdell is representing the Bay Area on a national stage.

name: Four Kings
description: The Four Heavenly Kings were four Cantopop crooners who ruled the Hong Kong airwaves in the ’90s. But the name of this Chinatown restaurant could easily refer to chef-owners Franky Ho and Mike Long and their partners, Millie Boonkokua, who runs front of house, and Lucy Li, who handles the books. These gender-nonspecific kings have been the undisputed monarchs of San Francisco since they set up shop below a Kumon last year, serving inspired Cantonese fare to lines of customers eager to pay fealty.  Not only is the food — XO escargot with milk bread, mapo spaghetti — delicious, but the energy is infectious; this is likely the most fun restaurant in the Bay Area. Long live the kings!

name: Rich Table
description: The teenage years can be awkward for restaurants. Too old to be the it girl of the moment, too young to be an elder statesman, establishments entering that pimply era can risk irrelevance or mediocrity. Not so Rich Table, which, 13 years after opening on the ground floor of a Victorian in Hayes Valley, remains one of the most delightful places to get a meal in San Francisco.  Service is both assured and warm, the food is craveable without playing it safe, and the convivial room hums with patrons who know just how lucky they are to be there.

name: Sun Moon Studio
description: If you’ve ever had occasion to visit the administrative and maintenance facility for the East Bay Municipal Utility District, you’ve been mere steps from the most exciting tasting menu in the Bay Area. Even more improbable than the location of West Oakland’s Sun Moon Studio is its staffing. In the kitchen are business partners and partner partners Sarah Cooper and Alan Hsu — and that’s it.  You won’t believe the food these four hands produce, particularly the house-made breads and pastries from baker Cooper that appear throughout the 12-course meal in dishes like a mini hot dog with lap cheong made by Hsu. The space is tiny and reservations are hard to come by, but keep at it. The wait is worth it.

name: Popoca
description: Popoca is a special place that honors Salvadoran tradition while carving out its own identity. Consider its luxe approach to pupusas: They’re made with heirloom corn, then stuffed with seasonal produce like charred Jimmy Nardello peppers and cooked over a wood flame.  Chef and owner Anthony Salguero started Popoca as a popup to explore his Salvadoran roots, then opened a brick-and-mortar in Old Oakland in 2023. Inside, the vibe is laid back; exposed brick walls and seafoam green tiles add stylish character. You’re going to want to start with a stellar Salvi-inspired cocktail before digging into banging sides like a scrumptious plum salad topped with tamarind granita and a phenomenal guacamole accompanied by tortillas brushed with asiento, pork lard with the sediment left from making chicharon. Then move on to a complex main, like the charred branzino over an exemplary sauce known as alguashte, made from pumpkin seeds, smoked fish carcass and a ton of spices. Popoca is an inspiration, propelling Salvi cooking to new heights. We challenge you to find another restaurant in the Bay Area, California, the whole country, like it.

name: The Progress
description: “Around here, we do things a little differently.” No doubt in 2014, when the Progress opened next door to its sister restaurant, State Bird Provisions, its dinner party format of shareable plates, graphically depicted on the menu as a Venn diagram, required some explanation.  Now that its concept is sort of just the way we eat, does the Progress feel stale? Anything but. Whether sidling up to the bar solo for a cocktail and off-menu hot dog or packing into the mezzanine with a dozen friends for a feast, the Progress continues to keep diners on their toes, applying flavor references and cooking techniques from around the world to California’s freshest and finest.

name: Copra
description: An undersung hero of the Bay Area’s booming contemporary Indian cuisine scene, Srijith Gopinathan returns to his upbringing with Copra, the crown jewel of the veteran chef’s growing empire. Here, Gopinathan gives the rambunctious flavors of Southern India and its neighboring Sri Lanka a palatial stage; woven chandeliers, vintage tropical wallpaper and a faux greenhouse lend grandeur to the dining room.  Grander still are the menu’s unabashed presentation of spice and the kitchen’s unmatched execution. Prime examples include the extravagant chutney platter, a wonderful distillation of smokiness, piquancy and coolness; the explosive rasam poori, delicate hollow puffs filled with passion-fruit-mint water; and melty black cod wrapped in banana leaves and crusted with shallots. No visit to Copra is complete without sampling dessert, especially the “Gods Own” Coconut Variation, an icy, multifaceted expression of the fruit. Of all the world-class Indian restaurants in the region, Copra is the most shining example.

name: Breadbelly
description: There are going to be some complaints that we put a bakery in the top 10. We hear you — there’s no table service at Breadbelly, and what tables there are are outside. But have you had the ma-faux-caccia? Or the lurid green kaya toast, flavored with pandan?  As good as they are, a Top 100 restaurant cannot stand on the strength of baked goods alone, but Breadbelly’s ever-changing menu of Asian-inflected soups, sandwiches and salads goes head-to-head with full-service breakfast and lunch spots. And it comes out on top.

name: SingleThread
description: From the front, the server is all silky poise, explaining each of the dozen tiny dishes arrayed on an undulating bed of moss, like a banquet for fairies. But from behind, he’s doing something weird with his hands. One hand is open, palm out, the other making a peace sign. Wordlessly, his colleagues alight at the table, pouring sparkling and still water as if by telepathy. This level of service is a signature of SingleThread’s California-kaiseki experience — an annoying buzzword, frankly, to describe a meal.  But since your trip to Healdsburg might begin with a visit to SingleThread’s farm and end at the inn upstairs, “experience”  feels apt. If you’re going to drop beaucoup bucks on an hourslong meal in Wine Country, it should be at SingleThread.

name: Zuni Café
description: There’s only one restaurant that has been on every single Top 100 list since its inception in 1996. It is, of course, Zuni, which, while technically a restaurant, has raptured into another category of San Francisco icons along with Emperor Norton and the wild parrots.  But that description risks doing a disservice to the food, now overseen by Anne Alvero but faithful as ever to the ethos of Judy Rodgers. Reading the menu — still printed daily with quirky indentations, like a modernist poem — you could be at Zuni in 1991, in 2008, or 2025. No matter the decade, when you’re at Zuni, you know you’re in for a special evening.

name: Tacos Oscar
description: Tacos Oscar has a talent for expanding the boundaries of the taco in eccentric and nonconformist ways. Admirably, about half of the menu is vegetarian, taking full advantage of California’s produce.  Tostadas become crunchy podiums for riveting salads or the juiciest tomatoes of the season. Crunchy charred broccoli regularly stars in tacos. On weekends, the taqueria has one of Bay Area’s best brunch services. You’ll find a petite, homey breakfast burrito and a phenomenal taco de huevo, where a crispy, runny egg is paired with smoky salsa macha and cheddar. Housed inside a shipping container, Tacos Oscar makes great use of its unconventional space, maintaining a convivial vibe with a rasquache-chic aesthetic. Tacos Oscar is the first true “Oakland-style” taqueria — a singular force that plays with the format and has loads of fun doing it.

name: Verjus
description: Owner Lindsay Tusk insists Verjus is a wine bar, not a restaurant. I’m not here to fight, Lindsay, but we don’t have a Top 100 Wine Bars list, so I hope you’ll understand. And while one could happily perch in the window with a glass of Champagne and spiced nuts and call it a night, why stop at snacks when you can have one of the most exquisite French meals in the city? Executive chef Walker Stern’s flawlessly executed fare — poulet Normande, a colorless omelette filled with Boursin, a dynamite bouillabaise-inspired crab soup — is a cut far above the requisite cheese plate offered at most wine bars. The candlelit space, refreshed after a four-year closure, is as glamorous as ever, and on weekends guest DJs spin vinyl to the sound of clinking glasses and satisfied sighs. — M.C.F.

name: Rose Pizzeria
description: It’s a difficult feat to be original as a pizza spot. Most tend to invest in a specific style: blistered Neapolitan, Midwestern tavern style, thin-crust New York, Californian sourdough. Rose Pizzeria is informed by all of those pizza cultures but forgoes all of the constraints; these Berkeley pies march to their own drum.  Each round is marked by a bubbly char and a crisp undercarriage, topped with what chef, co-owner and self-described “pizza man” Gerad Gobel calls “maximum impact” toppings, like ample chiles, funky sheep’s milk cheese and smoked mozzarella. Try the wild card She Wolf, a meet-cute pie where puttanesca and burrata find love over red sauce, though straightforward combinations are also excellent. The spicy Caesar salad is mandatory; the funk of miso and the aromatic kick of Calabrian chiles create a fun riff on an old favorite. Of course, the neighborhood restaurant lives up to its name with a patio surrounded by flowers.

name: Aziza
description: There’s no dearth of tremendous restaurants in the Richmond District, but most of them are on the humbler side — mom-and-pop pho shops, Russian bakeries, dim sum joints and the like. These neighbors make Aziza all the more wondrous.  For most of the last 20-odd years, Mourad Lahlou has been operating this quietly remarkable Moroccan restaurant, named after his mother, on the corner of 22nd and Geary across from a UPS store. It’s a date night destination, a place for a celebratory brunch (don’t miss the spongy Moroccan pancakes) and the pride of the neighborhood.

name: Snail Bar
description: There are many wine bars in the Bay Area, but none with food as punchy, or, frankly, as cool as Snail Bar in Oakland. The restaurant’s greatest strength is an element of surprise.  The ever-changing, genre-bending menu might include soft shell crab sandwiches one day, Colombian hot dogs or Venezuelan corn pancakes the next, and then umami-laden tomato toasts or ibérico ham pupusas. Food with that much flavor begs for a glass (or bottle) of sake or, the restaurant’s strong suit, natural wine. If you’re at all curious (or skeptical) about the wine style, Snail could turn you into a fan.

name: Soba Ichi
description: West Oakland is home to one of the Bay Area’s most important Japanese noodle shops: Soba Ichi, a master of the lithe grey noodles made from buckwheat. These threads are an artisan product made by Koichi Ishii, who mills buckwheat in-house; mixes the dough by hand; and cuts each noodle.  Those extra efforts give his product a one-of-a-kind springiness. They are either served warm in a soulful, soothing broth or cold with a potent dipping sauce on the side; this route helps preserve noodle texture. You’ll want something to snack on before the soba, and the crunchy croquettes or kakiage, assorted fried vegetables, are finger-licking good. Designed by Zen Buddhist priest and craftsman Paul Discoe, Soba Ichi is a magical place with a minimalist dining room engulfed in light wood. The slow trickle of a bamboo fountain on the patio creates a sense of peace. After just one visit, Soba Ichi leaves a lasting impression that will have you plotting your return.

name: Benu
description: With high-end Korean restaurants dominating the culinary conversation, it’s easy to forget the climate when Corey Lee opened Benu in 2010. It would be roughly a decade before “Parasite” would take home the Oscar for best picture and “Squid Game” would introduce the U.S. to dalgona candy. The members of BTS were in short pants. And in San Francisco, Korean food — like every other Asian cuisine save for sushi — was relegated to the cheap and cheerful. Benu changed all that.  Fifteen years later, Lee’s tasting menu is as thrilling as ever. It sure isn’t cheap, but you’ll be smiling from the moment the first bite, a century quail egg surrounded by technicolor green cabbage juice, hits the table.

name: Mijoté
description: Mijoté is a wine bar at a former sushi counter run by a Japanese chef who trained in Paris. Perhaps it goes without saying, but there’s nothing else like it in San Francisco.  The vibe is that of a neo-bistro, with a four-course set menu and a muscular wine program that skews toward the unfiltered and obscure. The generously portioned dishes show off chef Kosuke Tada’s kink for crunchy vegetables, fruit paired with fish and meat, and gorgeous sauce work. For natural wine heads visiting the Bay Area, it’s appointment dining.

name: Wah Jee Wah
description:
Editor’s note: Wah Jee Wah has closed and will reopen at a new location soon.
Chef Rounak Dumra nearly lost his business and his life to a stroke. But two years later, he and his Indian restaurant Wah Jee Wah in Hayward are stronger than ever. Driven by a passion to honor his late parents, Durma’s cooking nods to his mother’s coastal roots and his father’s penchant for the blazing arts of Indian barbecue. One power source of the restaurant is a charcoal grill, where tender lamb ribs, whole chicken wings or springy cubes of paneer sizzle and soak up the fragrant smoke. The best side in the house is a spiced masala mac, which reinvents the classic dish with fun and bold spice. Wah Jee Wah, with its backyard cookout vibe and communal picnic tables, has become a dining destination for Hayward, carving out its own hazy niche.

name: Quince
description: There are many — really so many — fine dining restaurants in the Bay Area where you can get an excellent meal. Quince is absolutely one of them. Chef Michael Tusk’s cooking, which relies heavily on produce from the farm he co-owns in Bolinas, is unassailably skilled. But it’s the thoughtful touches that surround Tusk’s food that catapult Quince into the tippy top tier.  Service is both kind and immaculate. There’s now a thoughtful cocktail program overseen by Thad Vogler of Bar Agricole. The interiors, new as of 2023, have been featured in Architectural Digest. The wine list slaps. While the price for the 10-course tasting menu puts Quince among the most expensive restaurants in San Francisco, there is also the option to dine à la carte at the bar or in the salon. It’s still not cheap, but it does mean a larger swath of diners can experience the sparkle of Quince.

name: Taishan Cuisine
description: People you might see at Taishan Cuisine at 2 a.m.: international students from China in head-to-toe Dior; the chefs behind James Beard-nominated Four Kings; a solo diner, earbuds in, taking down an entire clay pot of eel rice. San Francisco’s Chinatown used to be full of late-night spots like Sam Wo. Now there’s really just Taishan Cuisine.  But even if you’re not a night owl, there’s still good reason to visit — namely, that it’s among the tastiest Chinese restaurants in the city. The centerpiece of any meal should be the Taishan roast chicken, cooked to order. It’s served head-on and carved tableside, and by “carved” we mean a server wearing both cotton and latex gloves will rend the bird into manageable chunks with her hands.

name: Sandy’s
description: Restaurants with Bible-length menus could take a page from Sandy’s. Housed in a squat building in the Haight-Ashbury neighborhood, the sandwich shop focuses most of its energy on famed New Orleans-style muffuletta, a multilayered sandwich that’s sliced and served like a savory cake.  The meat option has ample richness, but so does the mushroom version, which radiates with umami; both feature a commanding olive and pepper spread, the sandwich’s key source of flavor that also brings balance. The menu has no skips, including the egg salad and Zapp’s chip sandwich, the pimento cheese and pickled okra sandwich, and one of the Bay Area’s best chocolate chip cookies. Chef Peterson Harter, who wears his heart on his sleeve, and the Sandy’s staff are engaging conversationalists, which makes the food even tastier.

name: Tacos Mama Cuca
full_address: 2075 Alum Rock Ave., San Jose, CA
description:
Editor’s note: Tacos Mama Cuca has relocated to San Jose. We have updated its location, though this write-up is based on its previous home in the East Bay.
Tacos Mama Cuca is a game changer: the first to embrace Sonoran cooking in the Bay Area. Initially a tortilla business in Oakland, Tacos Mama Cuca grew into a taqueria and popup before purchasing a food truck, now based in Hayward. It all starts with incredibly pliable flour tortillas, which are the perfect vehicle for charcoal-grilled beef — two major building blocks for the Sonoran diet. A phenomenal salsa selection, with heat levels ranging from moderate to searingly hot, make the tacos belt out arias of flavor and spice. Nonnegotiable is the jumbo taco yaqui, featuring a grilled Anaheim pepper stuffed with melted cheese and your choice of meat — go with asada or pastor. The best dish, however, is a snack, a flour tortilla smeared with spicy beans, which owner Maria Marquez gifts to any guests who ask, free of charge. Seductive scents of mesquite wood are a constant at Tacos Mama Cuca, making every visit feel like a backyard barbecue.

name: Sons & Daughters
description:
Editor’s note: Sons & Daughters has moved to the Mission District. We updated the location, but this write-up is based on its previous space.
You can feel Noma’s impact at restaurants throughout the Bay Area — a focus on foraging, a fondness for fermentation — but Sons & Daughters is the closest you will get to the pioneering Copenhagen institution in San Francisco. While chef Harrison Cheney hails from the United Kingdom, stints working with Icelandic and Norwegian chefs left their mark on his culinary style. Since assuming the helm at this 15-year-old restaurant in 2023, Cheney has transformed the tasting menu into a tribute to New Nordic cuisine. There are edible flowers, toasted hay and garums galore. The potent, acidic flavor profiles might be challenging for some, but dinner at Sons & Daughters is an exhilarating slice of Danish fine dining in our own backyard.

name: Bombera
description: Housed inside a former fire station, Bombera, Spanish for firewoman, has become one of the Bay Area’s best Mexican restaurants and one of Oakland’s hottest tables. The atmosphere is cheery, especially on the charming front patio, and a soundtrack of cumbias keeps the place in high spirits. Dishes like carrots over a reservoir of brothy beans, spiked with a smoky, fiery almond-chile salsa, and fire-roasted beets paired with a sour, hot pumpkin seed sauce encapsulate what the restaurant does best: contemporary Mexican cuisine that honors its foundations.  That’s further evidenced by a charcoal-burning grill, the source of aromatic toastiness. Handmade tortillas are full of depth, as they’re made from corn that was nixtamalized and ground in-house. That masa appears on the menu in myriad ways: as the base for flaky beer-battered fish tacos, folded into a triangle for tetelas or, simply, accompanying entrees like butternut squash topped with bold pecan mole. You’d be hard-pressed to find better moles in the Bay Area than the ones at Bombera.

name: Café Jacqueline
description:
Editor’s note: Café Jacqueline closed in December.
Plenty of places specialize in one specific dish — burgers, dumplings, croissants — but we only know of one that has built an entire business around soufflés. Sure, there are a few other items listed on Café Jacqueline’s menu, including a quite good onion soup, but the gravity-defying soufflés are the thing. Chef-owner Jacqueline Margulis, just shy of 90, has been perfecting her technique since she opened her North Beach restaurant in 1979. Although she now employs stand mixers after breaking her arm last year, not much else has changed. The guest checks are written by hand. To book a table, you call a landline. The soufflés, both savory and sweet, are artfully served tableside by Matthew Weimer, who, together with his prolific mustache, has been running front of house since the ’90s. Café Jacqueline is a love letter to eggs, air and craft, and there’s no other place like it. — M.C.F.

name: Friends Only
description: It’s difficult to not reach for hyperbole when discussing Friends Only, whose sushi omakase feels like an exercise of exaggeration. Somewhere in between dry-aged tuna belly nigiri and brain-melting uni handrolls, your cognitive functions begin to slow, your knees start to buckle and, by the end of it, your soul may even transcend.  Friends Only is as much a lab of experimentation as it is a restaurant; it’s a local pioneer of aged fish, a technique that greatly improves texture by slowly extracting water content. In the midst of nigiri courses you might get delicate crab rice blanketed in black truffles or a lush seafood chawanmusi that’s so rich the government might give it a tax break. Walking into Friends Only feels like being in on a secret. After all, only 20 lucky people per night are allowed to embark on a journey through the sea. This is better than finding Atlantis — if you can afford it.

name: Californios
description: Californios presents a vision of Mexican cuisine that reaches new peaks. Everything about Val M. Cantú’s San Francisco fine dining restaurant, from the menu to the ambience to the beverage list, radiates elegance.  Diners are surrounded by dark walls and colorful art as they embark on a two hourlong journey filled with mole, flame-kissed meat and the infinite geometric formations of heirloom corn masa. (The bulk of the multicolored masas are made in-house, except the blue version, which comes from maiz savant Bolita.) If you’ve ever been curious about Mexican wine producers, Californios stocks a fair number of them, as well as the first sake produced in Mexico. Desserts by pastry chef Kelli Huerta are a high point; past highlights include nostalgic, bite-size Choco Tacos and a superb chocolate tamal.

name: State Bird Provisions
description: We lament the disappearance of traditional cart service from most of the Bay Area’s dim sum restaurants. Ordering exclusively from a menu isn’t nearly as fun as selecting from piled-high steamer baskets or petite dishes wheeled around to your table (and for that matter, whatever happened to dessert trays?).  But you’ll still find the carts at State Bird Provisions, a dim sum restaurant that is neither traditional nor, for that matter, Chinese. Since 2012, State Bird Provisions has been one of San Francisco’s most fun restaurants, referencing the cuisines of Mexico, Japan, Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe and China in small plates that are distinctly Californian.

name: Delfina
description: The first time I tasted Delfina’s spaghetti, I realized that the versions I’d previously experienced were meager pantomimes. Here, each pasta skein is the dictionary definition of al dente; the pomodoro sauce is luscious and tangy, with a hint of spice. The dish feels like reconnecting with an old friend. Delfina excels at this art of reintroduction, which has seemed especially apt since the 26-year-old Cal-Italian trailblazer remodeled a few years ago. Inspired by timeless Italian architecture, the reworked dining room is striking; booths are leather-bound and the archway is copper-plated. Signature dishes like the grilled calamari over creamy beans and the marinated anchovies continue to impress. Before the remodel, co-owner Craig Stoll, who has a spaghetti tattoo on his arm, considered shuttering the restaurant, but realized that he wasn’t “ready to stop cooking this kind of food.” Let’s hope he never is. — C.H.

name: Atelier Crenn
description: Dominique Crenn is the most famous chef in San Francisco. She was the first woman in the United States to receive two and, subsequently, three Michelin stars; she’s been named to the Time 100; and she married an actual movie star, setting an unrealistic example for chefs everywhere. Many might book a table at her flagship restaurant to participate in chef worship, but make no mistake — this is serious food, not a vanity project from a celeb.  In a region where the dominant cooking style is local and seasonal ingredients, simply prepared, Crenn is unafraid to bust out the liquid nitrogen and agar-agar. But her modernist leanings coexist with her commitment to sustainability at the restaurant, which serves no meat, limited dairy and has banned single-use plastics.

name: Ok’s Deli
description: The sandwiches at Ok’s Deli are akin to the films of an auteur director. The superb, craggy Sichuan-style fried chicken gives you innovation. The pate-swiped banh mi, an ode to Oakland’s Vietnamese delis, shows perspective.  The hot coppa and mozzarella displays technical form. And the sisig bolillo — fatty pork head in a crisp, bubbly roll — demonstrates mastery. Chef Albert Ok self-distributes his edible art via a pint-sized, ultra-casual deli. These sandwiches are worthy of an Oscar, a Golden Globe and two thumbs up from Siskel and Ebert.

name: La Ciccia
description: When the owners of La Ciccia announced they were selling in 2022, the pasta-eating public collectively held their breath. For 16 years, the Sardinian restaurant had not just been the favorite of Noe Valley locals, but also the likes of food media cognoscenti like Mark Bittman. A deal was brokered and then fell through. From the wreckage of that sale, an unlikely hero emerged: Cheryl Maloney, the real estate broker who had handled the negotiations, decided she would buy the restaurant herself.  Under her ownership, little has changed about La Ciccia, from the menus written in Sard to the sea urchin pasta with cured tuna heart, and that’s a very good thing.

name: San Ho Won
description: San Ho Won looks something like a boutique bunker, with concrete walls and sturdy wood beams. Step inside and you’ll become absorbed into its world, where chefs Corey Lee and Jeong-In Hwang apply exacting precision to Korean barbecue.  Roasted flesh over coals is the restaurant’s most express talent, whether that’s tender, toothy short ribs or pork ribs varnished with savory-sweet black bean paste. The menu is rounded out with fortifying stews, classic Korean sides and slam-dunk appetizers. You’ll want to order the egg soufflé, a cloud of steamed custard that’s moments away from taking flight. Down to the final spoons of fluffy bingsu, San Ho Won keeps its proverbial foot on the gas, never forfeiting a moment to impress.

name: Kajiken
full_address: 112 S. B St., San Mateo, CA
description: The best seat in the house at Kajiken is in front of the noodle room. It has the power to send you into a trance as you watch balls of dough become the strings found in each bowl of ramen. Don’t gawk too hard, though, it’s not a one-way mirror.  The San Mateo noodle shop specializes in aburasoba, a rare, dry-style ramen featuring the aforementioned springy, house-made noodles, and toppings like raw egg yolk, minced pork, sliced beef or sesame paste. Without the broth, the flavor of each bowl is concentrated, and the noodles retain a superb bounce. You’re guaranteed to encounter a line during peak hours, but it’s worth the wait.

name: Madcap
full_address: 198 Sir Francis Drake Blvd., San Anselmo, CA
description: I’m going to get emails about this, but scouting for restaurants in Marin is a challenge. I’m not saying there aren’t good restaurants in Marin, but when placed next to comparable spots elsewhere in the Bay Area, they often don’t come out ahead. I mention this not to neg on Marin but to ensure there’s no misunderstanding about Madcap: Chef Ron Siegel’s restaurant is not just “Marin good,” it’s anywhere good. There’s no grading on a curve with his Japanese-inflected tasting menu, which is also quite reasonably priced compared to fine dining restaurants in the city or Wine Country. Advantage Marin! — M.C.F.

name: Minnie Bell’s Soul Movement
description: Despite the seismic changes to the Fillmore, the spirit of the historic Black neighborhood lives on through Minnie Bell’s Soul Movement, which became San Francisco’s best soul food restaurant the moment it opened.  Upon entering the cheery, casual restaurant, a black-and-white image of the Fillmore takes you to the past while murals honor chef-owner Fernay McPherson’s inspirations in the kitchen, her grandmother and great-aunt. Minnie Bell’s holds the championship belt for the best mac and cheese in the Bay Area, besting all its foes with cheese two ways: as a gooey pasta binder and a crispy cheese crust. The scabrous fried chicken, delicately perfumed with rosemary, is exceptional too, especially with a few drops of housemade hot sauce; think vinegary Louisiana-style, only fresher and spicier.

name: Mägo
description: Mägo, a singular Colombian restaurant in Oakland, has none of the stuffy air you might associate with fine dining. The dining room is colorful, matching Colombia’s flag, and the attitude is loose and calm. The sounds of Latin American pop hits gently hang in the air as dinner unfolds. Chef Mark Liberman explores his Latin American roots in a fabulous (and competitively priced) tasting menu.  He reinterprets nostalgia with finesse, crowning medallion-sized arepas with a half moon of compressed fish head; stuffing corn pancakes with cheese followed by a drizzle of brown butter sauce; or crowning fluffy fried yuca with a dollop of whipped black beans. Main courses lean on grilled fish or meat, with a smokiness earned from a wood-burning, steering wheel-operated hearth. If your budget allows, consider the dynamic drink paring — pours of wine, sake and other surprises that bring out lovely hidden notes in dishes.

name: Flour + Water
description: Sixteen years after Thomas McNaughton opened his Cal-Italian restaurant on a quiet corner in the Mission, he and co-chef Ryan Pollnow oversee a mini Flour + Water empire.  There’s Flour + Water Pasta Shop, sister restaurant Penny Roma, one Flour + Water Pizzeria with more on the way, and a line of packaged pasta that can be found at a grocery store near you. With all the expansion, is the original Flour + Water still a worthy destination? Very much so. It’s still one of the hardest reservations in San Francisco, and its pasta tasting menu one of the best special occasion splurges.

name: La Vaca Birria
description: From down 24th Street, the whiff of mesquite smoke finds its ways to your nostrils, compelling your feet to investigate the source. You’ll find it at La Vaca Birria. The bright newcomer represents the natural evolution of a Mission District taqueria, honoring past traditions of the barrio while introducing new ones, like a woodburning grill.  Burritos are top-notch, best when filled with beef, oyster mushrooms or chicken, which are each freckled with char and imbued with woody aromas. Equally flavorful is the beef birria, which is braised for several hours in a panoply of spice, then paired with milky mozzarella cheese for quesabirria tacos that are best in show. The taqueria has a bubbly, bright colored aesthetic, with pastel tones, a wall of roses and a veil of dangling ropes by the kitchen.

name: Joodooboo
description: From its beginnings as a simple tofu and banchan shop, Joodooboo has blossomed into one of Oakland’s most thrilling restaurants, focusing on the gentler side of Korean cuisine. Banchan are conduits for the seasons: Spring brings marinated snap peas and artichoke, summer supplies sweet corn swirled with tofu, fall produces persimmon kimchi, and winter delivers braised kabocha squash.  Side dishes like these play an Oscar-worthy supporting role in the fried dooboo set, an excellent way to experience Joodooboo’s dreamy homemade tofu. Sometimes time is an ingredient. That’s the case at Joodooboo, which has grown gradually into a one-of-a-kind place to eat.

name: Bo Ne Phu Yen
description: This restaurant is a constant reminder of the astounding quality and specificity of Vietnamese cuisine in east San Jose. Bo Ne Phu Yen, a stall inside the Lion Plaza food court, is a steak and eggs savant.  While the restaurant serves other food, the main draw is succulent bo ne, a French-Vietnamese dish served on a sizzling cow-shapped platter. It consists of fried eggs, slightly sweet pork meatballs, rich pate and melty filet mignon, garnished with cilantro sprigs and crumbled peanuts. This all-inclusive meal comes with a side of piquant soy sauce, a cup of tea and a crispy, toasted roll, ideal for dipping into runny yolks, smearing with pate and soaking up the beef drippings. The food court is constantly buzzing with shoppers, and Bo Ne is one of the most popular food options, so try to find a seat as soon as you order.

name: Scoma’s
full_address: 1965 Al Scoma Way, San Francisco, CA
description: Scoma’s should be a tourist trap. It’s been located on Fisherman’s Wharf since 1965, and its wood-paneled dining rooms, covered with autographed photos of celebrities from the ’70s, overlook the water. You are likely to find yourself seated next to tourists, whether they’re in from Melbourne for a couple of weeks or from Modesto for the day. But there’s no trap here.  Scoma’s, now owned and operated by the second and third generation, doesn’t take your patronage for granted. White-jacketed career servers are as helpful as they are knowledgeable, the fish is fresh off the boat every day and the Bloody Marys hit just right. It’s the type of place where you take family when they’re in town as a treat — ostensibly for them, but really for yourself.

name: Fikscue
description: Texas wishes it had barbecue like Fikscue in Alameda, where customers brave hourslong lines for a spectacular blend of Indonesian cooking and Lone Star State-style smoked meats. Where else can you find hulking dino ribs and smoked brisket rendang?  The idea for the “Indo-Tex” combination started at the dinner table of married owners Fik and Reka Saleh. Fik, who previously ran a barbecue popup, nails the Texas technique with his wobbly, bark-encrusted brisket, chewy beef back ribs and snappy jalapeño-cheese sausages. Meanwhile, Reka, who used to run an Indonesian popup, puts Fikscue in a league of its own with her Indonesian offerings like the rendang, cubed beef tossed with a complex paste made of red pepper and coconut milk, and soto padang, a spiced noodle soup, to which you can add a slab of brisket. Eat your heart out, Texas.

name: Lily
description: World-class, inexpensive pho and banh mi shops abound in the Bay Area, but chef Rob Lam set out to do something different with Lily. His $72 fried rice, draped with caviar, uni and Wagyu, was a sly wink to his ambitions that, much to Lam’s distress, became a runaway success. It’s no longer on the menu, which is just as well; Lily’s less blingy dishes, like its bouncy rice noodle rolls or the dill-showered turmeric fish dish known as cha ca La Vong, are delectably dead serious.  There may be no $12 pho on the menu, but Lily’s Friday through Sunday lunch special — $32 for an appetizer, entree and fun beverage — is one of the top deals in the city.

name: LeYou
full_address: 1100 N. First St., Suite C, San Jose, CA
description: There are few joys as great as dining at LeYou in San Jose, where you’ll find distinct spins on Ethiopian cooking, matched with sips of honey wine or craft Ethiopian beer. To understand what it brings to the table, look no further than the fun appetizers like the kitfo bites, spicy, raw marinated tuna served with crisped, tangy injera.  Many Ethiopian spots offer a similar menu, but you’ll find deep cuts at LeYou like kay sir, beets toasted with onions and peppers; butcha, savory mashed chickpeas; and sauteed okra. Combination platters are like a greatest hits album and the most comprehensive way to approach the menu. Start with a vegetarian sampler, which includes four sides or stews, then, if needed, order a full-size entree of spiced white fish, springy mushrooms or saucy doro wot chicken. Dining here is especially enchanting when you sit on the patio, surrounded by trees, as the taste of spicy, gingery berbere dances on your tongue.

name: HK Lounge Bistro
description: Everyone loves a comeback story. In 2019, Annie Ho’s Hong Kong Lounge II, one of the most respected dim sum palaces in San Francisco, went up in flames after utility workers struck a gas line outside. The staff managed to shepherd 50 customers through the kitchen to safety as the front of the restaurant burned. The building is still boarded up, but in 2023, Ho began a new chapter in SoMa.  HK Lounge Bistro is humbler than Hong Kong Lounge II, with drop ceiling tiles and an entryway that is reminiscent of a dentist’s office. But the walls are the same signature salmon pink and, most importantly, the dumplings and bao are as good as ever. So too is the hospitality, something of a rarity in the efficient world of dim sum. On a Sunday morning or in a time of crisis, they take care of their customers.

name: Nari
description: In 2014, Pim Techamuanvivit burst onto the scene with Kin Khao, changing the way the Bay Area thought about Thai food. Her 2019 follow-up, Nari, showcases the way the Bay Area changed her.  Local produce stars in dishes like artichoke laab, massaman curry with cherries, and som tum with kohlrabi and Pink Lady apples. The whole space, with industrial bones softened by abundant greenery, is sexy, but for date night privacy, retire to the tucked-away cocktail bar upstairs for concoctions that incorporate pandan and lemongrass.

name: Troubadour Bread & Bistro
description: Melissa Yanc and Sean McGaughey are power players in Healdsburg’s restaurant scene, and Troubadour contains their many talents. During the day, the restaurant is a sandwich-focused cafe, showcasing the breadmaking skills of the couple, who also run the popular Quail & Condor bakery. By night, the restaurant becomes a luxe French fine dining joint with a seven-course menu.  You might start with a bite-size croque madame, then move onto red wine-braised snails and citrusy roast duck. Freshly baked bread is dispensed liberally to sop up saucy drippings.

name: Vik’s Chaat
description: Vik’s Chaat has been the Bay Area’s standard bearer for Indian food for over 30 years. Housed in a warehouse, Vik’s is both a grocery store and a restaurant, with canary-yellow walls and a cafeteria-style setup.  There’s a humility about the place: Orders are called out over an intercom; disposable sporks are the only available utensils; and metal trays hold thalis, rotating multicomponent meals including a main, a chutney, achaar (pickles), flatbread and basmati rice. Vik’s is a top source for exploring the elaborate world of chaat, savory Indian snacks such as dahi batata puri, hollow wheat puffs glazed with cool yogurt and sweet tamarind chutney, or sev puri, crackers topped with potatoes, mangos and a yellow confetti of crispy bits.

name: Valley
description: We should all be so lucky as to have a Valley in our town. The multihyphenate business supplies Sonoma with coffee and pastries in the morning, lunch in the afternoon, dinner and/or drinks in the evening. In need of a bottle of wine? Or of Valley’s house brand olive oil, for that matter, or perhaps some candles or a picnic blanket or a bar of quality chocolate? You can get that too.  If it sounds like it’s doing a bit much, sit down to a meal and see how good simplicity can taste. Fall figs roasted in olive oil spooned over a wedge of French cheese, black pepper-dusted tomatoes on mayo-slicked bread — it’s the type of food that makes you wonder, why is the version I make at home not half as tasty?

name: Mariscos El Charco
full_address: 1925A Alum Rock Ave, San Jose, CA
description: Pleasure and pain are what Mariscos El Charco does well. The bright blue truck in San Jose specializes in the superb seafood culture of Mazatlán, the coastal city in the Mexican state of Sinaloa. Its spicy secret weapon? Chiltepin, a tiny ball-shaped chile with fast-acting incendiary properties that will loosen your sinuses and eye ducts, producing tears of joy and torture.  The fiery pepper, mixed with lime juice and black pepper, electrifies the scallop aguachile — a botana ideally shared with a group. Individual diners, meanwhile, should order tostadas topped with shrimp ceviche or, better yet, a mix of fish, octopus and scallops known as the chapito. Sit at one of the outdoor communal tables equipped with tostadas and a half dozen bottled hot sauces.

name: Barberio Osteria
description: There are more Cal-Italian restaurants in San Francisco than there are children, so the saying goes. Did we really need one more? If Barberio Osteria is that restaurant, then yes.  Open for less than two years within spitting distance of two other Cal-Italian heavy hitters on this list, Barberio Osteria flies slightly under the radar but is no less deserving of accolades for its handmade pastas, respectful use of local produce and oxtail-stuffed fried olives showered with cheese. It may be the new kid on the block, but both front and back of house have the confidence of an old-timer.

name: 3 Bottled Fish
description: You can almost taste the passion at Vietnamese café 3 Bottled Fish in Oakland. Chef-owner Paulette Tran’s style is fun and improvisational, which means the menu changes weekly. The dishes are so compelling that you’ll be eager to return and see what she’s up to next.  On one visit, you’re met with banh mi, layered with tender steak and eggs on crusty sourdough; the next time, you might encounter an aromatic beef rice porridge. If you’re really lucky, you’ll be graced with salmon belly com tam with skin that crackles like bruleed sugar. The vibe is relaxed, the kind of place where you’re encouraged to thumb through books, strike up a conversation with locals waiting for takeout or stare at the TV showing an infinite loop of Vietnamese street vendors.

name: Tanzie’s
description: Brunch can be one-note and predictable. Not at Tanzie’s, where the genre is roused with considerable Thai flavor. The café, with its mint green decor, is intimate, unpretentious and comfortable, like a family member’s living room.  Inspired by the food culture of the northern Thai city of Chiang Mai, flavors skew sour and spicy, borrowing from its bordering neighbor, Laos. Those leanings are encapsulated in aromatic, bouncy sausages, which pair fabulously with “lava eggs,” a runny, open-faced omelet. Ribbons of pork jowl melt in your mouth. And sizzling platters of fried rice, coated in peppery curry paste, pack a savory wallop. Dinner service is the most recent addition to its repertoire, and similar to brunch, it looks beyond the typical Thai fare, with dishes featuring beef cheek, pork blood and brains.

name: Ettan
description: Ettan walked so that Copra could run. Chef Srijith Gopinathan’s first collaboration with businesswoman and heiress Ayesha Thapar, filled with greenery and chandeliers, set the template for Copra’s open, airy design. With second-story tables overlooking an atrium, it’s a dramatic setting for Desi families celebrating special occasions or couples on dates.  The Cal-Indian menu makes excellent use of local produce, incorporating lacinato kale into saag paneer and substituting persimmon in dishes that might otherwise feature mango during the winter months.

name: Cotogna
description: Kim Kardashian loves Cotogna. No wait, don’t go! You too will love Cotogna, even if you’re not a professional famous person.  Yes, the dishes are Instagrammable — the egg-yolk filled raviolo that sauces itself like one of those British cakes; the focaccia di recco, or “salty cheesy bread” as Kardashian called it, with the epic cheese pull — but they’re not gimmicks. More accessible than Quince, less edgy than Verjus, this is the restaurant from Michael and Lindsay Tusk that charms one and all.

name: Mingala
description:
Editor’s note: Mingala appears to have closed.
Mingala is a criminally underrated Burmese and Malaysian restaurant that lives and dies by the funk. Much of its finest offerings are titillating because of the frequent use of fermented shrimp paste, giving extra umph to sauteed veggies, sauces and salads. Here, you’ll find the Bay Area’s greatest rendition of tea leaf salad, which is juicy, nutty, crunchy and funkier than a Herbie Hancock jam sesh. Don’t skip the beef (or chicken) rendang, which is glazed in a seriously thick gravy. All that flavor is matched by a stylish, spacious dining room.

name: La Taqueria
description: To borrow from video game parlance, La Taqueria’s burritos have the most balanced skill tree in the Bay Area. It ticks several boxes: It’s historic, pleasing bellies since 1973; it’s unique, eschewing rice in its burritos; it’s consistent; and it’s affordable.  A line is almost guaranteed in the evening, but La Taq is worthy of patience. You will want to stick to carne asada, which is smoky and tender with a little chew. Ask for your burrito dorado, which means the glorious bundle will be seared on all sides until the wheat casing becomes dappled and crisp. La Taq is a culinary diplomat for the Mission, showcasing the strength of its game-changing burrito culture.

name: Liholiho Yacht Club
description: We spent a remarkably long time trying to figure out how to categorize Liholiho Yacht Club. Hawaiian was our first thought, but chef Ravi Kapur rejects the term, which he reserves as a descriptor for food indigenous to the islands. Californian, then? There’s local produce aplenty, but the menu and vibes are too tropical for it to feel like a proper fit. Asian felt plausible, but we landed on American — a bit of a copout but also, we argue, accurate. What’s more American than a menu by a Native Hawaiian Chinese Indian chef featuring longanisa, rendang curry and salsa macha?  In a region that commodified and exoticized Polynesian culture through tiki bars like Trader Vic’s, this nuanced approach to the food of modern-day Hawaii feels like a reclamation. The large-format photo that hangs above the bar isn’t of an anonymous Hawaiian beauty; it’s Kapur’s mom, mid-laugh, her hair flying in the wind.

name: Kin Khao
full_address: 55 Cyril Magnin St., San Francisco, CA
description: Since 2014, Kin Khao has been a cunning linguist in the fiery language of spice. It was powerhouse restaurateur Pim Techamuanvivit’s introduction to the world as well as the city’s first Thai restaurant to attain a Michelin star.  Admittedly, the decor veers functional over flashy, but the unrelenting flavors are where extravagance lies. The restaurant has a way of producing magnificence out of the ordinary. Wings become crunchy delicacies, doused in a funky, spicy tamarind sauce that sets your taste buds ablaze, while cranberry beans transform into slightly crisped vessels of creaminess, heat and acidity. The noodle soup, gently flavored with aromatic five-spice, is crowned with a gnarled, crispy duck leg, while the sumptuous massaman curry features beef cheek that’s braised until it practically falls apart.

name: Cultured Pickle Shop
description: Ferment emporium Cultured Pickle Shop may be the final evolution of Berkeley cuisine: super seasonal, a little hippie and wholly original. On weekends, the production kitchen serves a three-course menu centered on the most ornate rice bowl you will ever encounter.  You might see a whopping 15 toppings: jewels of aged leafy greens, root vegetable kimchis, citrusy pickles, miso-braised cruciferous produce. Beverages, too, are wonderfully funky, like yuzu kombucha or warm amazake, a fermented rice drink, flavored with ginger. Try to score a seat inside, so you can see all the forthcoming experimentations: clear jars filled with cloudy live cultures, releasing tiny bubbles of gas as they slowly breathe.

name: Lou’s Takeaway
full_address: 621A Del Ganado Road, San Rafael, CA
description: It’s best to approach Lou’s Takeaway, a wickedly flavorful, eccentric takeout-only shack in San Rafael, with an open mind. Chef and owner Anthony “Lou” Rizzi draws inspiration from a number of Asian cuisines, coupled with California seasonality. The menu changes frequently, sometimes on the day of service, and items sell out quickly.  One week, there might be chilled noodles with chicken, only to vanish and be replaced by a ceviche salad, then melty, pork in black vinegar caramel over perfectly cooked rice. You don’t go to Lou’s for a stable menu. You go because you can always rely on Lou’s to deliver something engaging and original. To avoid crushing disappointment, get there early or call to check menu availability. Most patrons grab their boxes and dash, however, if you want to eat there, a J-shaped yellow bar and a handful of stools are up for grabs.

name: Mister Jiu’s
description: When I visited Mister Jiu’s last year, I was seated facing a table capped by a man shouting into his phone. “I’m FaceTiming you from the only Michelin-starred Chinese restaurant in the United States!” he gushed. I couldn’t even be mad. Mister Jiu’s is a pilgrimage site for Chinese Americans who care about food (... all of us?); Brandon Jew is our local boy who made good. Since earning that Michelin star in 2017, Jew has opened a chain of fast-casual restaurants and mentored a small army of chefs who have gone on to open other businesses, including some on this list. He’s also pivoted to a tasting menu, lending Mister Jiu’s a more formal air, men on cellphones notwithstanding. But you can still order à la carte — and walk in — at the bar. It’s my favorite part of the restaurant, where you’ll find a convivial, lively atmosphere of diners ripping apart scallion milk buns and licking honey butter off their fingers between sips of jujubee negroni. — M.C.F.

name: Prubechu
description: An argument could be made that Prubechu belongs on this list because it’s “important.” When it opened in 2014, it introduced Chamorro cuisine — the food of people indigenous to Guam and the Mariana Islands — to a broad audience. To our knowledge, there’s no other restaurant like it in the continental United States.  But while Prubechu is certainly a restaurant of cultural importance, it’s also a restaurant of just plain deliciousness. The Aloha-shirted waitstaff will be happy to provide you with a detailed introduction to the barbecued, lemon-marinated chicken kelaguen or the coconutty caramelized potato soup, but even if you heard none of it, you’d still have a meal you’d be thinking about for a long time.

name: Peña’s Bakery
description: There’s a particular brand of sandwich artistry at panaderia Peña’s Bakery that you won’t find elsewhere in the Bay Area. These tortas are graced with homestyle reverence — the best of which is chorizo con papas. While the mix might sound ordinary on paper, Maria De La Luz Briones, the Oakland bakery’s de facto chef, makes the rich swirl of potato and spiced sausage a day in advance to concentrate its flavor.  She piles the filling into soft telera bread — baked fresh daily — with avocado and serves it with a side of potent chile de arbol salsa. If chorizo is not your thing, the cheese torta, stuffed with thick pads of queso fresco, is an excellent vegetarian alternative. Peña’s sandwiches are a favorite of the Fruitvale neighborhood’s day laborers, but anyone with taste buds can tell you that these delicacies taste like beholding a Renaissance painting.

name: Azalina’s
description: When Azalina Eusope, who comes from a long line of Malaysian food vendors, first started selling her kaya-glazed banana fritters at farmers markets in the Bay Area, she was continuing in the footsteps of forebears. With her restaurant in the Tenderloin, she’s come a long way from vending from behind a folding table.  Each course of her reasonably priced set menu, which changes every four weeks, is inspired by a different ethnic group that calls Malaysia home. There might be Cantonese-style duck or a Malay laksa lemak, but of particular note are dishes representing Eusope’s own Mamak people, a Muslim minority group originally from Southern India. Her concept is as ambitious as her cooking is formidable.

name: Outta Sight Pizza
description: Outta Sight Pizza is a Tenderloin community jewel with a bohemian, skater energy. Virtually every inch of wall space inside the slice shop is decorated with art, skateboards and shelves full of trinkets, tchotchkes and thingamajigs. The bulk of the pies are thin-crust New York-style, with toppings ranging from pepperoni or olives to something eccentric like persimmons.  Order a slice of them all, especially the (vegan) puttanesca, a carpet of tangy red sauce littered with briny olives and capers, or the school lunch-esque Lunch Lady slice, a nostalgic cheese-and-sauce square. The pizzeria also excels at sandwiches, like the crunchy morty melt, a stack of mortadella, molten cheese and a most scintillating basil pesto.

name: Kitchen Istanbul
description: If I visit Kitchen Istanbul on a Sunday or Monday, days when many restaurants in the city are closed, I’m apt to see some familiar faces. Is that the general manager of Quince? Didn’t that person sell me a bottle of wine at Saison? This neighborhood spot in the Inner Richmond is a restaurant professional’s restaurant thanks to owner Emrah Kilicoglu’s effortless hospitality and impeccable wine list. Together with wine director Joseph DiGrigoli, Kilicoglu focuses on small producers, often with low-intervention farming and bottling practices. Ask DiGrigoli or Kilicoglu to pair something interesting with the charcoal-grilled adana kebab, and save room for the rice pudding, served with a blackened skin like a Basque cheesecake. In my line of work, it’s impossible to be a regular at any one place, but if I could be, I’d want to be a regular here. — M.C.F.

name: Kunjip
description: Kunjip is a fixture in Santa Clara’s formidable Korean food scene — and just about the most regal place to eat bone broth. The dining room walls look like marble and are lined with plush booth seating; the floors are the color of brick; and a beam of light traces the perimeter of the ceiling, making the light-wood paneling glow.  There are about a dozen items on the menu, but the main draw is milky seolleongtang, for its slippery rice noodles and nourishing beef broth made with oxtails. However, cold rice noodles over an invigorating sweet and piquant sauce is the sleeper hit. Kunjip is busiest on weekends, when the strip mall crowds with eager families hoping to get their soup on, so expect to wait up to half an hour.

name: Cyrus
description: If you were to imagine a fine dining experience in Wine Country, you might envision white tablecloths in a charming, rustic farmhouse overlooking a vineyard. Cyrus has the vineyard, but the building is more Bond villain HQ than quaint country manor.  Designed by starchitects Olson Kundig, responsible for such projects as the renovation of Seattle’s Space Needle, the building is as elemental to the meal as the food, beverages and service. Canapés are served in the bar area overlooking the vines. Diners then progress into the kitchen, where they are invited to wander between courses served at a horseshoe-shaped communal table. The heart of the meal takes place in the dining room — no white tablecloths in sight — and a parting sweet gift is dispensed in a zany, walk-in-closet-sized room with a chocolate waterfall.

name: Z & Y
description: Somewhere in the bubbling cauldron before you, under that layer of chiles, is fish. You’ll have to go searching for the filets with a ladle, nudging aside the brick red peppers, but they’re there.  If the thought of this flaming chile oil sends you into an anticipatory sweat of excitement, Z & Y is the place for you. In many of its signature dishes, there is plenty of má — the numbing tingle of Sichuan peppercorns — to complement the là of spicy peppers, but the spice averse will find concessions made here and there.

name: Psychic Pie
description: Cross my palm with silver and your fortune I will tell. The mists of the astral realm are clearing, and I see in your future … ranch. Lots of very good, herby ranch for you to dip crusts of pizza into. Yes, pizza.  There will be an abundance, as much as you wish, sold to you by the inch, like in the old days of Rome. Pepperoni and four cheese, but also asparagus, nettle-pistachio pesto and beets on a sourdough crust. But wait, I am seeing your spirit guides have more in store for you. You will manifest soft serve made of the lushest buffalo milk from Double 8 Dairy, drizzled with local olive oil and sprinkled with salt from the sea. All this and more will be yours at Psychic Pie!

name: 10 Butchers
full_address: 595 E. El Camino Real, Sunnyvale, CA
description: Watching beef marbled with a web of fat sizzle on the grill is mesmerizing fun at upscale Korean barbecue restaurant 10 Butchers. The atmosphere is something like a fancy butchery meets steakhouse, except the energy is turned to, well, 10.  Premium meats are a forte of the restaurant, ranging from shaved beef tongue to thick pork jowl and A5 wagyu cuts. The staff cooks at your table, scissor-slicing meats with surgical precision into bite-size morsels.
